questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: State the force-extension relationship before the limit of proportionality.

questions.stem: A spring is stretched by small forces and then returns to its original length. What does Hooke’s law say about the force and extension before the limit of proportionality is reached?
questions.question: A spring is stretched by small forces and then returns to its original length. What does Hooke’s law say about the force and extension before the limit of proportionality is reached?
options: The force is directly proportional to the extension.
The force is inversely proportional to the extension.
The extension is independent of the force.
The force is proportional to the original length only.
questions.correctAnswer: The force is directly proportional to the extension.
questions.explanation: Hooke’s law applies before the limit of proportionality: force and extension are directly proportional.

distractorRationales.text: The force is inversely proportional to the extension.
The extension is independent of the force.
The force is proportional to the original length only.

questions.senNote: Checks: State the force-extension relationship before the limit of proportionality.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Recall the Hooke’s law equation.

questions.stem: Which equation links force, spring constant and extension for a spring within its limit of proportionality?
questions.question: Which equation links force, spring constant and extension for a spring within its limit of proportionality?
options: \(F = k \div e\)
\(F = e \div k\)
questions.explanation: For a spring obeying Hooke’s law, \(F = ke\), where \(F\) is force, \(k\) is spring constant and \(e\) is extension.

distractorRationales.text: \(F = k \div e\)
\(F = e \div k\)

questions.senNote: Checks: Recall the Hooke’s law equation.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Calculate force using Hooke’s law.

questions.stem: A spring has a spring constant of 25 N/m. It is stretched by 0.12 m. What force is needed?
questions.question: A spring has a spring constant of 25 N/m. It is stretched by 0.12 m. What force is needed?
questions.explanation: Use \(F = ke\). The force is \(25 \times 0.12 = 3.0\,N\).

distractorRationales.misconception: centimetres or powers of ten confused

questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate force using Hooke’s law.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Calculate extension by rearranging Hooke’s law.

questions.stem: A spring constant is 40 N/m. A force of 6.0 N is applied. What is the extension of the spring?
questions.question: A spring constant is 40 N/m. A force of 6.0 N is applied. What is the extension of the spring?
questions.explanation: Rearrange \(F = ke\) to \(e = F \div k\). The extension is \(6.0 \div 40 = 0.15\,m\).
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate extension by rearranging Hooke’s law.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Find extension from original and stretched length.

questions.stem: A spring is 15 cm long before loading and 23 cm long after a load is added. What is the extension?
questions.question: A spring is 15 cm long before loading and 23 cm long after a load is added. What is the extension?
options: 8 cm
38 cm
23 cm
15 cm
questions.correctAnswer: 8 cm
questions.explanation: Extension is stretched length minus original length: \(23 - 15 = 8\,cm\).

distractorRationales.text: 38 cm
23 cm
15 cm

questions.senNote: Checks: Find extension from original and stretched length.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Calculate spring constant from force and extension.

questions.stem: A force of 12 N produces an extension of 0.040 m in a spring. What is the spring constant?
questions.question: A force of 12 N produces an extension of 0.040 m in a spring. What is the spring constant?
questions.explanation: Rearrange \(F = ke\) to \(k = F \div e\). The spring constant is \(12 \div 0.040 = 300\,N/m\).
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate spring constant from force and extension.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Recall the unit of spring constant.

questions.stem: What is the unit of spring constant in Hooke’s law?
questions.question: What is the unit of spring constant in Hooke’s law?
options: N/m
N m
m/N
kg/m³
questions.correctAnswer: N/m
questions.explanation: Spring constant is force per metre of extension, so its unit is newtons per metre, \(N/m\).

distractorRationales.text: N m
m/N
kg/m³

questions.senNote: Checks: Recall the unit of spring constant.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Interpret the straight-line section of a force-extension graph.

questions.stem: A force-extension graph for a spring is a straight line through the origin for the first few readings. What does this show?
questions.question: A force-extension graph for a spring is a straight line through the origin for the first few readings. What does this show?
options: Force is directly proportional to extension.
Extension stays constant as force increases.
The spring is already plastically deformed.
The spring constant is zero.
questions.correctAnswer: Force is directly proportional to extension.
questions.explanation: A straight line through the origin shows direct proportionality between force and extension.

distractorRationales.text: Extension stays constant as force increases.
The spring is already plastically deformed.
distractorRationales.misconception: straight section confused with plastic deformation

distractorRationales.text: The spring constant is zero.
distractorRationales.misconception: zero intercept confused with zero gradient

questions.senNote: Checks: Interpret the straight-line section of a force-extension graph.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Use a force-extension graph to find spring constant.

questions.stem: On a force-extension graph, a force of 8.0 N gives an extension of 0.020 m in the straight-line region. What is the spring constant?
questions.question: On a force-extension graph, a force of 8.0 N gives an extension of 0.020 m in the straight-line region. What is the spring constant?
questions.explanation: The gradient of a force-extension graph is \(F \div e\). Here \(8.0 \div 0.020 = 400\,N/m\).

distractorRationales.misconception: extension divided by force then scaled incorrectly

questions.senNote: Checks: Use a force-extension graph to find spring constant.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Identify the limit of proportionality on a force-extension graph.

questions.stem: A force-extension graph is straight at first and then begins to curve. What does the point where the graph first stops being straight represent?
questions.question: A force-extension graph is straight at first and then begins to curve. What does the point where the graph first stops being straight represent?
options: The limit of proportionality.
The original length of the spring.
The point where force becomes zero.
The mass of the load.
questions.correctAnswer: The limit of proportionality.
questions.explanation: The limit of proportionality is where force and extension stop being directly proportional.

distractorRationales.text: The original length of the spring.
The point where force becomes zero.
The mass of the load.
distractorRationales.misconception: load variable confused with graph point

questions.senNote: Checks: Identify the limit of proportionality on a force-extension graph.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Describe behaviour beyond the limit of proportionality.

questions.stem: A spring has been stretched beyond its limit of proportionality. Which statement is now most likely?
questions.question: A spring has been stretched beyond its limit of proportionality. Which statement is now most likely?
options: Force and extension are no longer directly proportional.
The spring constant must be exactly zero.
Extension must decrease when force increases.
The original length is equal to the force.
questions.correctAnswer: Force and extension are no longer directly proportional.
questions.explanation: Beyond the limit of proportionality, the graph is no longer a straight line through the origin, so Hooke’s law no longer applies.

distractorRationales.text: The spring constant must be exactly zero.
Extension must decrease when force increases.
The original length is equal to the force.

questions.senNote: Checks: Describe behaviour beyond the limit of proportionality.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Describe elastic deformation.

questions.stem: A rubber band returns to its original length after a small stretching force is removed. Which term describes this behaviour?
questions.question: A rubber band returns to its original length after a small stretching force is removed. Which term describes this behaviour?
options: Elastic deformation.
Plastic deformation.
Resultant deformation.
Thermal deformation.
questions.correctAnswer: Elastic deformation.
questions.explanation: Elastic deformation means the object returns to its original shape and length when the force is removed.

distractorRationales.text: Plastic deformation.
Resultant deformation.
Thermal deformation.

questions.senNote: Checks: Describe elastic deformation.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Recognise plastic deformation after excessive stretching.

questions.stem: A spring is stretched too far and does not return to its original length when the load is removed. What has happened?
questions.question: A spring is stretched too far and does not return to its original length when the load is removed. What has happened?
options: It has been plastically deformed.
It has obeyed Hooke’s law perfectly.
Its extension has become zero.
Its mass has become zero.
questions.correctAnswer: It has been plastically deformed.
questions.explanation: Plastic deformation is permanent; the object does not return to its original length after the force is removed.

distractorRationales.text: It has obeyed Hooke’s law perfectly.
distractorRationales.misconception: permanent change confused with elastic behaviour

distractorRationales.text: Its extension has become zero.
Its mass has become zero.

questions.senNote: Checks: Recognise plastic deformation after excessive stretching.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Explain why original length is measured in the force-extension practical.

questions.stem: In a force-extension practical, why should the original length of the spring be measured before adding loads?
questions.question: In a force-extension practical, why should the original length of the spring be measured before adding loads?
options: So extension can be calculated after each load is added.
So the mass of the spring can be ignored.
So the spring constant is automatically zero.
So the graph does not need axes.
questions.correctAnswer: So extension can be calculated after each load is added.
questions.explanation: Extension is the loaded length minus the original length, so the original length is needed for every extension calculation.

distractorRationales.text: So the mass of the spring can be ignored.
So the spring constant is automatically zero.
So the graph does not need axes.

questions.senNote: Checks: Explain why original length is measured in the force-extension practical.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Calculate extension from practical measurements.

questions.stem: A spring has original length 10.0 cm. With a 2.0 N load its length is 13.5 cm. Which row should be recorded for extension?
questions.question: A spring has original length 10.0 cm. With a 2.0 N load its length is 13.5 cm. Which row should be recorded for extension?
questions.explanation: The extension is \(13.5 - 10.0 = 3.5\,cm\), paired with the applied force of 2.0 N.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate extension from practical measurements.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Explain why repeated readings improve a force-extension practical.

questions.stem: A student repeats each extension reading three times in a spring practical. Why is this useful?
questions.question: A student repeats each extension reading three times in a spring practical. Why is this useful?
options: It helps identify anomalous readings and calculate a mean.
It makes the spring constant change each time.
It means no graph is needed.
It removes the need to measure force.
questions.correctAnswer: It helps identify anomalous readings and calculate a mean.
questions.explanation: Repeats improve reliability because anomalous results can be spotted and a mean extension can be calculated.

distractorRationales.text: It makes the spring constant change each time.
distractorRationales.misconception: repetition confused with changing material property

distractorRationales.text: It means no graph is needed.
It removes the need to measure force.

questions.senNote: Checks: Explain why repeated readings improve a force-extension practical.

A horizontal line.

A vertical line.

The spring has lost all stored energy as mass.

The force was always zero.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Calculate work done from the area under a force-extension graph.

questions.stem: A force-extension graph is a straight line from 0 N at 0 m to 10 N at 0.20 m. What is the work done in stretching the spring?
questions.question: A force-extension graph is a straight line from 0 N at 0 m to 10 N at 0.20 m. What is the work done in stretching the spring?
questions.explanation: The work done is the triangular area under the graph: \(0.5 \times 10 \times 0.20 = 1.0\,J\).

learningObjective.statement: Distinguish mass from weight in a different gravitational field.

questions.stem: An astronaut takes a 70 kg equipment case from Earth to the Moon. Which statement is correct?
questions.question: An astronaut takes a 70 kg equipment case from Earth to the Moon. Which statement is correct?

The case has no mass on the Moon.
The case has the same weight everywhere in space.
